query: gold standard for evaluating nasal obstruction

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Obstruction -- History and Physical -- Anterior Rhinoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to assess for septal deviation, hypertrophy of the nasal turbinates, or nasal polyposis during anterior rhinoscopy. A quick test to prove choanal patency can be performed by asking the patient to exhale nasally onto a cold metal spatula (nasal misting test).

retrieved_docs.title: Velopharyngeal Insufficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Nasometry measures nasal emission and calculates a ratio between nasal and oral sound emissions (nasalance) using a sound separator between the nose and upper lip. The ratio is compared with normative values. Preoperative and postoperative nasalance can also be compared to determine surgery success. However, nasometry does not localize or quantify the velopharyngeal gap. Large velopharyngeal gaps can produce artificially low nasometric scores due to diminished high-pressure airflow. [3] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nasal -- Related Testing -- Rhinomanometry
retrieved_docs.content: Rhinomanometry is used to evaluate the respiratory function of the nose by measuring pressure and flow during normal respiration. Obstruction will lead to higher than expected pressures and decreased flow. Two primary methods are used:  anterior or posterior. Anterior rhinomanometry takes unilateral measurements of one nostril at a time, whereas posterior rhinomanometry evaluates both simultaneously. Anterior rhinomanometry is more commonly used, and primarily evaluates on which side of the septum the obstruction arises.

retrieved_docs.title: Septoplasty -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Several scoring systems are available to grade nasal obstructive symptoms. The Nasal Obstruction Symptom Evaluation (NOSE) scale is a validated instrument used to evaluate the degree of obstruction. Those with low scores are unlikely to benefit from surgery. [8] [9] It is important to take a thorough history to establish whether concomitant factors may contribute to or cause obstruction, such as trauma, rhinosinusitis, allergies, vasculitis, illicit drug use, chronic use of decongestants, autoimmune disease, or malignancy. In these cases, adequate medical therapy (e.g., intranasal corticosteroids for chronic allergic rhinitis) should be offered prior to considering surgery. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Choanal Atresia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The hallmark finding on physical examination is nasal obstruction or stenosis. Anterior rhinoscopy should be performed, although persistent rhinorrhoea can make assessing the posterior nasal cavity challenging. Examination is essential to rule out other conditions that can mimic choanal atresia, including severe septal deviation, turbinate hypertrophy, nasal polyposis, and nasal tumors. Placing a dental mirror beneath each nostril to check for fogging during exhalation can be particularly helpful, especially after using a topical nasal decongestant. Nasal endoscopy is considered the gold standard for diagnosis, as it distinguishes between conditions mimicking choanal atresia and determines the degree of stenosis in cases with partial involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Obstruction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of acute upper airway obstruction, evaluation should be prompt, and the provider should be prepared to proceed to intervention with limited delay. All necessary airway equipment should be made available at the time of evaluation in case immediate intervention is required. The nasopharyngeal area may be evaluated with a flexible or rigid endoscope. Direct laryngoscopy is another tool that may also be helpful not only for diagnosis but also in case of intervention. Imaging techniques are available, but extreme caution should be utilized in patients with an acute obstruction or chronic obstruction with acute distress. Obtaining imaging should not delay the correction of obstruction in patients in acute distress. In these cases, imaging may be helpful in determining etiology after the obstruction has been alleviated. Imaging modalities that are used to assess airway obstruction include the lateral head and neck x-rays, CT scans, and MRI. CT scans produce images that can assess both bony structures and soft tissues. The airway diameter can be evaluated, as well. The latest CT scans are fast and can create three-dimensional images. Again caution must be undertaken when deciding if the patient is stable for such diagnostic testing. MRI can also be quite helpful in determining the etiology of airway obstruction. MRI is of particular benefit when evaluating soft tissue masses and surrounding structures. Besides generating three-dimensional images, MRI does not produce radiation, and dye is not always used. The limitations of MRI are availability and cost. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Acute Rhinosinusitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: History and physical examination are crucial to diagnosing RARS. In most cases, healthcare providers can accurately diagnose RARS by evaluating the duration and progression of symptoms, along with applying the major and minor diagnostic criteria set outlined by the American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery. [18] Patients exhibiting unilateral disease without septal deviation, experiencing severe and debilitating symptoms, or those whose symptoms persist despite appropriate empirical treatment should undergo nasal endoscopy for further evaluation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dacryocystorhinostomy -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Nasoendoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Nasoendoscopy must be included in the preoperative workup. This is essential when an endonasal DCR technique is being considered, but also important even if an external DCR is planned. A decongestant plus anesthetic spray (oxymetazoline and lidocaine) gives the best decongestion and anesthesia in nasal endoscopy. [21] A 30 degree, 2.7 mm slim nasoendoscope is introduced into the nose. The anterior nose is observed for polyps or other abnormalities. The endoscope is then advanced, and the following observations are made: Is the septum straight, or does it deviate? Deviation towards the operative side may make access difficult and require a septoplasty. Is the mucosa healthy? Can the middle turbinate be identified? Is there sufficient space to perform endoscopic DCR? Are there any other abnormalities that may hinder surgery?

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Sinusitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Acute sinusitis is a clinical diagnosis. Conventional diagnostic criteria for rhinosinusitis in adults require the patient to have at least 2 major symptoms or 1 major plus 2 or more minor symptoms. The criteria for children are similar, but there is greater emphasis on nasal discharge rather than nasal obstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinoplasty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patient-reported outcomes include validated instruments such as the Standardized Cosmesis and Health Nasal Outcomes Survey, Rhinoplasty Outcomes Evaluation, and Nasal Obstruction Symptom Evaluation questionnaires. These tools seek to quantify postoperative quality of life changes and are arguably the most important measures of a surgery's effectiveness. [11] [12] [13] Consequently, patient selection is considered the most critical predictor of operative success and the most effective means of avoiding litigation or the need for secondary surgery, which is notoriously complex.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nasal -- Related Testing -- Acoustic Rhinometry
retrieved_docs.content: Acoustic rhinometry is primarily to measure the cross-sectional area and length of the nose and nasal cavity. It uses sound waves and measures reflection in the nasal cavity. Evaluation of nasal cavity geometry and abnormalities is possible. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Olfactory Testing -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no known complications of olfactory testing. However, the olfactory testing procedure may be tiring for patients as complete olfactory testing can take up to 30 minutes. Following international guidelines, anterior rhinoscopy and nasal endoscopy should be done as part of the ENT evaluation to rule out intranasal pathology, potentially impeding the sense of smell. [3] The complications of these procedures have been reviewed before. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Olfactory Testing -- Potential Diagnosis -- Olfactory Dysfunction Secondary to Sinonasal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: This is 1 of the most common causes of olfactory dysfunction and is often caused by rhinosinusitis (acute: < 12 weeks versus chronic ≥ 12 weeks) with hyposmia or anosmia as an important symptom besides rhinal symptoms (such as nasal discharge, pressure-like pain in the face and nasal obstruction). [15] [16] The olfactory decline occurs gradually and is not associated with parosmia. [17] ENT exam may reveal nasal polyps and inflammation of the mucosa of the nose. Olfactory testing typically shows diminished odor threshold and discrimination but normal odor identification. [2] In addition to the olfactory testing, computer tomography of the skull base and paranasal sinuses may help document polys and other alterations of the sinuses, which are not visible with the clinical examination.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Polyps -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, the diagnosis of CRSwNP is confirmed at this stage, and the patient should be directed toward appropriate medical management. Patients whose symptoms do not improve with adequate medical therapy will need further evaluation with a PNS CT scan. Furthermore, patients with unilateral symptoms or findings should undergo imaging studies as soon as possible. Surgery is considered an alternative for patients with an established diagnosis of CRSwNP who are refractory to medical management. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Obstruction -- Evaluation -- Oxygen Saturation
retrieved_docs.content: Oxygen saturations are an essential first step in assessing a patient with airway obstruction, as they can be a marker of the severity of the obstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients older than 5, nasal nitric oxide measurements can be used as a screening or diagnostic tool for diagnosing primary ciliary dyskinesia. [23] Paranasal sinus epithelium produces nitric oxide, and nasal nitric oxide measurements are typically extremely low (less than 100 nL/min) in primary ciliary dyskinesia patients. Decreased nasal nitric oxide values can also be seen in acute viral respiratory infections and patients with cystic fibrosis. Therefore, patients should fully recover from viral illnesses, and cystic fibrosis should be ruled out before performing this test. [24] No absolute threshold has been validated as a cut-off value for nasal nitric oxide, which makes it somewhat challenging to standardize across centers. Sensitivity and specificity vary depending on the cut-off value used. One study, which used 30 nL/min as a cut-off, reported a sensitivity of 91% and specificity of 96% in diagnosis. [21] Therefore, while this is a good screening test, confirmation testing is required to characterize the abnormal ciliary ultrastructure and function further.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: The term “nos” is classified as a child of “other upper respiratory disease,” and “other upper respiratory disease” in turn is a child of the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere). The clinical procedure “control of epistaxis” is performed directly at the site identified as “nos” and its primary focus is “الرُعاف” (epistaxis). Additionally, “الرُعاف” is recognized as a child condition within the overarching group “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر.” This hierarchy links the nonspecific upper respiratory condition (“nos”) to a specific nasal disorder category and highlights that the management of epistaxis targets the “nos” site while addressing the condition “الرُعاف,” which itself belongs to the same broader nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Epistaxis (nosebleed) is classified as a specific type of nasal disorder that falls under the broader category of “nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere,” indicating that it is considered a child condition within this unspecified nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2936
retrieved_docs.content: الرُعاف (epistaxis) is classified as a child entity within اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere), indicating that it is a specific type of nasal disorder that does not fit into other subcategories. Clinical attention to this condition centers on the control of epistaxis, which is identified as a primary focus of management for patients experiencing nasal bleeding. Recognizing الرُعاف as a distinct subset of unclassified nasal disorders facilitates targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at effectively controlling bleeding and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Other upper respiratory disease is categorized under the broader group of nasal disorders not elsewhere classified (اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Within this same nasal‑disorder category, epistaxis (الرُعاف) is identified as a specific condition. This hierarchical organization shows that both the general classification of other upper respiratory diseases and the particular manifestation of nosebleeds are subsumed by the overarching nasal disorder category, facilitating systematic diagnosis, documentation, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.content: clinicians to select the most appropriate steroid formulation for upper‑airway (allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis) and lower‑airway (asthma, COPD) inflammatory conditions, optimizing therapeutic efficacy and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1173
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with اِضطِراب أنفي, indicating that these interventions address nasal disorders. Within this broader category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of procedure (inverse_isa) that falls under Procedures on Nose. The control of epistaxis procedure is performed directly on the nos, meaning it targets the nose itself. Together, these relationships illustrate that managing nosebleeds is a direct, targeted intervention on the nose and forms part of the wider set of procedures used to treat nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2698
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a therapeutic procedure that is performed directly on the nose (nos) and serves as the clinical focus of the Arabic term الرُعاف, which denotes a nosebleed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر also has الرُعاف as a child concept, reinforcing the connection between unspecified nasal disorders and bleeding from the nose. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network linking nasal‑septum disorders, their classification within upper‑respiratory disease taxonomy, and the corresponding diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those aimed at controlling epistaxis—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing nasal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SEND Non‑Neoplastic Finding Type Terminology includes a subset concept identified as “other upper respiratory disease.” Within this classification, the Arabic term اِضطِراب أنفي (nasal disorder) is defined as a member of the “other upper respiratory disease” category, reflecting an inverse‑is‑a relationship that places the nasal disorder under this broader respiratory grouping. Additionally, the concept “Procedures on Nose” is linked to اِضطِراب أنفي, indicating that nasal procedures are clinically related to the management or assessment of this specific nasal disorder. Together, these relationships illustrate how the terminology framework organizes nasal disorders within upper respiratory disease categories and connects them to relevant procedural interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2622
retrieved_docs.content: NASAL PREPARATIONS represent a broad class of pharmaceutical formulations intended for administration via the nasal route, and within this class the specific subgroup of nasal decongestants for systemic use is included. Nasal decongestants for systemic use are a particular type of NASAL PREPARATIONS that are formulated to relieve nasal congestion while also exerting systemic pharmacologic effects, highlighting the diversity of therapeutic options encompassed by nasal drug delivery.
